title: Do Large Language Models Prefer Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Policies?
text:
Background

Executive Order 14319 — “Preventing Woke AI in Federal Government” (July 23, 2025)
Labels DEI as the most “pervasive and destructive” influence on AI systems
Rapid growth in AI adoption across government institutions
5,397 use cases reported by 23 federal agencies in 2024–2025

title: Do Large Language Models Prefer Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Policies?
text:
Research Question

Do large language models systematically prefer diversity, equity, and inclusion policies over merit-based alternatives?

title: Do Large Language Models Prefer Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Policies?
text:
Conventional Approach
Do you prefer DEI policies?

Yes / No

Do you prefer DEI principles in hiring policies?

Yes / No

Which policy do you prefer?

DEI-based policies vs. Performance/merit-based policies

Each LLM receives these two policy packages and is asked to select its preferred one.
Because attributes are randomly assigned across packages, we estimate the causal effect of each attribute on the LLM’s choice using the Average Marginal Component Effect (AMCE)

title: Do Large Language Models Prefer Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Policies?
Do Large Language Models Prefer Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Policies?
text:
Identification Strategy


The B problem

T → B: template framing induces unmeasured latent features B (tone, rhetoric)
B → Y: B affects LLM output
Without controlling for T, B can bias Z estimates

Solution: three-tier nesting



Tier
Model
Purpose




1
chosen ~ Z
Baseline


2
chosen ~ Z + C
+ Context


3
chosen ~ Z + C + T
+ Template (blocks B)






Identification check

If \(T\) matters → coefficients change
If \(T\) doesn’t matter → coefficients stable


OLS linear probability model with HC2 robust standard errors (Hainmueller et al. 2014).
Estimated separately per LLM

title: Do Large Language Models Prefer Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Policies?
section: Main Result: AMCE All Five LLM
text: Main Result: AMCE All Five LLM

title: Do Large Language Models Prefer Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Policies?
Do Large Language Models Prefer Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Policies?
text:
Key Findings


All five LLMs prefer DEI screening and tie-breaking — unanimous, large effects. Screening AMCE: +0.14 to +0.53 depending on model.
Quota preference is the critical fault line — effects span −0.44 (Gemini) to +0.06 (GPT). Same prompt → opposite advice depending on which model you use.
Context matters systematically — Afghanistan shifts all models toward quota acceptance; USA shifts all toward quota aversion. LLMs encode country-specific norms from training data.
Template blocking validates the design — Core Values AMCE rises 4–7pp when template is controlled (Tier 1 → Tier 3), confirming that T → B → Y was real and Tier 3 is needed.
DEI diversity ranking: GPT-4o-mini (0.98) > Llama (0.85) > Claude (0.81) > DeepSeek (0.61) > Gemini (0.15).

title: Do Large Language Models Prefer Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Policies?
text:
Implications



LLMs are not neutral advisors on DEI policy
Choosing a model is itself a normative design decision, especially for quota-related questions
Country context in prompts substantially shifts recommendations



Conjoint + AMCE design is well-suited for probing LLM normative preferences
The three-tier DAG strategy (T blocks B) is important for unbiased Z estimates

title: Do Large Language Models Prefer Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Policies?
text:
Thank You

Research question
Do LLMs prefer DEI hiring policies?
Answer
Yes — but it depends on the model, the attribute, and the country context.

Hanif Ullah Sajid
hanifwrites@gmail.com

Jason Windett
jwindett@charlotte.edu



LLMs & DEI Policies

title: Hanif Ullah Sajid
text:
Welcome!
I am currently a Postdoctoral Research Fellow at the Center for North Carolina Politics & Public Service at Catawba College, in collaboration with the School of Data Science at UNC Charlotte, working with Dr. Micheal Bitzer and Dr. Jason Windett. I am also affiliated with Politics, AI, & Data Science (PANDAS) lab and have been affiliated with the Public Opinion, Learning, and Sentiment Lab (POLS-Lab). I earned my Ph.D. in Public Policy from the University of North Carolina at Charlotte, where I pursued my studies as a Fulbright Scholar.
I use artificial intelligence, particularly large language models (LLMs), natural language processing (NLP), and machine learning to explore and solve complex problems in the social sciences. My primary research interests focus on research methods and comparative agendas for tracking policy activities. I specialize in computational social science, quantitative text analysis, and survey experiments. I am passionate about developing new interfaces, building effective data collection and annotation pipelines across disciplinary boundaries, and integrating diverse methods to better understand political and policy processes.

title: Software for Good
text:
gais: Generative AI Suite
gais is a Python package that provides a unified and consistent interface for working with multiple LLM providers. It also enables local LLMs via Ollama and Fireworks. It provides more flexibility with respect to responses and API configuration compared to its sister package,  azllm .
Citation: Sajid, H.(2025). gais (Version 0.1.1) [Computer software]. https://github.com/hanifsajid/gais
For more information, visit the Documentation.
azllm
azllm is a Python package that provides a unified interface to work with multiple LLM providers including OpenAI, DeepSeek, Grok, Gemini, Meta’s LLaMA, Anthropic, Ollama, and more.
Citation: Sajid, H., Radford, B., Dai, Y., & Windett, J. (2025). azLLM (Version 0.1.2) [Computer software]. https://github.com/hanifsajid/azllm
For more information, visit the Documentation.
Legiscan Search Tool:
A Streamlit-based web application that enables users to search, filter, and download U.S. legislative data using the LegiScan API. The tool supports searches across all state legislatures and the U.S. Congress, with advanced filtering options by bill status, chamber, type, and date range. Results can be easily exported in CSV or JSON format.
Try online application: LegiScan Search Tool.
View the source code on GitHub:, LegiScan Search Tool Repository.
lvoteview
lvoteview is an R package that enables local access to voteview data without restrictions on the number of documents per request. It allows queries on any available field in the database. The package also exports all intermediate functions used in creating a pscl::rollcall object, offering flexibility for customization or reuse. Additionally, it includes polarity measurement, which can be used as an argument in wnominate to assess ideal points. (under development)
